⚛️ On March 4, seven companies (Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft, OpenAI, Oracle and xAI) signed a document at the White House containing a critical clause: they committed to "build, bring, or buy" all the new generation their data centers need. And to contribute to the cost of the capacity reserved for them, even if they end up not consuming all of that electricity ⚡️ That day mattered more than we realised, and it is the key to reading what media reports today, because it sets the logic that the entire energy build-out of AI is going to follow. And that logic is very important for the #uranium thesis. Hugely important. 👉 The 🇺🇸 United States has struggled for decades to build new #nuclear capacity. One of the fundamental reasons: very few buyers were willing to backstop the capex. 👉 Now some of the most creditworthy companies on the planet are accepting that they will finance the new generation and the infrastructure their data centers will require, including part of the capacity they may never consume. 💥 Reuters reports today that a second round is coming: utilities, data center developers and state governors will be invited to take on similar commitments. Why the hurry? Because the household electricity bill is becoming the political ceiling on the development of AI. The Ratepayer Protection Act is already moving through the House of Representatives, months away from the midterms. Some context: OpenAI alone appears to be talking internally about needing 30 GW by 2030. That is roughly 30% of the entire US nuclear fleet operating today. You cannot add load on that scale without raising the bill for existing customers, unless whoever creates the new demand also finances the new generation and the grid upgrades needed to serve it. 🔥 Gas will probably absorb the first wave, although turbine order books already stretch into the 2030s. In other words, gas, usually the fastest answer to an immediate jump in power demand, is running into bottlenecks of its own. When what you need is a 20-year contract, relatively stable prices, firm generation and carbon-free electricity, the options at scale narrow considerably. Nuclear is one of the most obvious answers ⚛️ A year ago the debate was whether data centers should pay for their own electricity. We have moved on: now we are discussing how much they should pay, what infrastructure they should finance, and under which tariff structure. I see a lot of uranium investors getting impatient because the spot price will not move. From where I stand, they are not paying enough attention to the tectonic shifts happening underneath: slow, but immense and brutal 💥💥💥 $URNM $URA $CCJ $NXE $UEC $DNN $EU $URG

Ol Kalou: End of Campaigns The campaign period in Ol Kalou ahead of Thursday's parliamentary by-election is officially over. Nine contestants will be on the ballot paper in a race that has seen money and goodies as key factors. #NTVTonight @Ben_Kitili @NBrygettes

— Joined fellow Nyanza leaders in Nyando Constituency to support hardworking Nyando Women engaged in small enterprises. Women are the economic backbone of many families in our region and in Kenya; where their struggle daily to raise whole families. Alongside support for the businesses, we are also constructively engaging our people to protect and defend the transformative gains we are witnessing under the broad-based government forged by ODM-UDA alliance. These grassroots engagements will continue as we strengthen our party’s bases and ready ourselves for the next polls — next year. TUKO TAYARI.
